name: KGCompass
maintainer: GLEAM Lab (Yang Boyang, Ren Jiadong, Jin Shunfu, Liu Yang, Liu Feng, Le Bach, Tian Haoye — institutional affiliation: [uncertain])
license: [uncertain] — GLEAM-Lab/KGCompass license not confirmed from available sources; GLEAM-Lab's other repos (CREF) use MIT
last_major_release: [uncertain] — paper submitted March 2025; GitHub repo exists at GLEAM-Lab/KGCompass; no formal versioned release confirmed
activity: [uncertain] — research prototype released alongside March 2025 paper; star count and commit velocity not confirmed from search results
storage_backend: Neo4j (with APOC 4.4 and GDS 2.6.8 plugins). The repository-aware knowledge graph is stored in and queried from a Neo4j instance; Docker Compose configuration with a dedicated Neo4j service is provided for reproducibility.
representation_primitive: entity+edge (knowledge graph). Node types: Issue, PullRequest, File, Class, Function. Edges link repository artifacts (issues/PRs) to codebase entities (files, classes, functions) and capture structural code relationships. Multi-hop traversal over this bipartite artifact-to-code graph is the primary retrieval mechanism. This is the only system among the three that natively represents both issue-tracker artifacts and code-structure entities in a single unified graph.
api_shape: Python library + CLI scripts; no MCP server; no REST API. Docker Compose environment provided for end-to-end reproducibility. No first-party MCP server.
hosting: self-host only; Docker Compose setup with Neo4j and GPU-enabled Python container
llm_dependency: retrieval (LLM used for patch generation in the Reader step; also for path-guided repair prompting). Ingest is LLM-free — graph construction parses repository metadata (issues, PRs) and code structure (AST) without LLM calls. Models used in evaluation: Claude Sonnet 4 (58.3% SWE-bench Lite), Claude 3.5 Sonnet, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen2.5 Max.
schema_extensibility: fixed — schema (Issue, PullRequest, File, Class, Function nodes + linking edges) is hardcoded. No YAML configuration or LLM-driven schema extension.
schema_richness: 5 node types: Issue, PullRequest, File, Class, Function. Edge types include: Issue/PR→File (mentions/modifies), Issue/PR→Class, Issue/PR→Function, File→Class (contains), File→Function (contains), Class→Function (contains), Function→Function (calls — inferred). The schema is intentionally narrow but uniquely bridges the issue-tracker and code-structure domains. Multi-label: not described. Multi-graph: not described. By count (5 nodes, ~7 edges) it is below Gimle §5's 20+/15+ target, but it is the only schema that provides the issue-PR-to-symbol chain that Gimle §5 explicitly requires.
temporal_model: none — snapshot-based. Graph represents repository state at repair time. No bi-temporal metadata, no event log, no valid_from/to. Issue/PR timestamps are stored as node properties but not used for temporal validity queries.
forgetting_policy: none — static snapshot; rebuild required to reflect new issues or code changes
ingest_latency_class: batch-offline — full repository parsing, issue/PR metadata extraction, and Neo4j graph population. Involves AST analysis and cross-referencing issue text with code entities (likely via text matching / LLM-free heuristics). Typically minutes per repository.
retrieval_modes: graph-traversal (multi-hop) — primary mode. Starting from an Issue or PullRequest node, the system traverses edges to reach candidate File, Class, and Function nodes. No vector similarity, no BM25. Neo4j Cypher queries implement the multi-hop traversal; APOC and GDS plugins support path algorithms.
retrieval_agenticity: multi-hop (2+ hop graph traversal is the defining mechanism — 89.7% of successful localizations required multi-hop paths with no explicit hint in the issue text). The traversal is deterministic/rule-based, not LLM-guided. Path-guided repair prompting attaches the traversal path as a reasoning chain to the LLM's repair prompt, making the retrieval result interpretable.
retrieval_budget_control: yes, partially — candidates are pruned to the top 20 most relevant functions before the LLM repair step. No explicit token-budget or hop-count parameter exposed as a first-class API; the top-20 cap is a fixed design choice.
multi_project_namespace: none documented — designed for single-repository instantiation. Neo4j could support multi-project partitioning via node labels or database namespaces, but this is not described in the paper.
multi_user_access: none — research framework; no access control
embedder_options: none — KGCompass is entirely embedding-free. Graph construction and traversal are structural/lexical; no vector embeddings are used.
llm_client_options: any LLM via prompt-based interface — KGCompass constructs a structured context (the top-20 candidate functions + path reasoning chains) and passes it to an external LLM for patch generation. Evaluated with Claude Sonnet 4, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen2.5 Max. No built-in LLM client; LLM is pluggable. Ingest can run without any LLM.
cost_privacy: LLM-free ingest means no API token spend during graph construction. Repair cost driven by the downstream LLM call on a ~20-function context. Paper reports $0.20 per repair on SWE-bench Lite with Claude Sonnet 4 at 58.3% resolution. Code and issue data processed locally via Neo4j; only the final repair prompt (containing code snippets) is sent to the LLM API.
code_artifact_linkage: issue-PR-to-symbol-chain (primary and distinctive strength — linking Issues and PRs directly to File, Class, Function nodes). symbol (function/class) — first-class nodes. file-path — first-class node. No line-level granularity. No AST-node-level detail. The issue-to-symbol chain is the deepest cross-domain linkage available among all three surveyed papers.
code_analysis_features: Symbol index (first-party — Class and Function nodes). Call graph (inferred, between Function nodes). File-to-symbol containment (first-party). Issue/PR text to symbol cross-reference (first-party, via text matching against code entity names). No diff-awareness. No import resolution. No type-awareness. Language scope: Python-focused for SWE-bench; broader language support depends on AST parser.
memory_os_layer: plaintext — constructs structured context (candidate function list + graph path reasoning chains) injected into the LLM prompt as text. No parametric or activation-tier components.
self_improvement_mechanism: none — static graph; no feedback loop from repair success/failure to graph construction or retrieval tuning
provenance_standard: custom-log (implicit) — the path-guided repair mechanism preserves the multi-hop traversal path as a reasoning chain attached to each candidate, providing human-readable provenance for why each function was selected. No W3C-PROV or OpenTelemetry.
benchmark_results: SWE-bench Lite: 58.3% resolution rate with Claude Sonnet 4 (single LLM, no multi-agent). Function-level fault localization accuracy: 56.0% (best among open-source approaches). Cost: $0.20 per repair. Relative LLM lift: +50.8% on Claude Sonnet 4, +30.2% on Claude 3.5 Sonnet, +115.7% on DeepSeek-V3, +156.4% on Qwen2.5 Max vs. pure-LLM baselines. Multi-hop finding: 89.7% of successful localizations had no explicit location hint in the issue text.
strengths: 1. Only system among the three that natively represents issue/PR artifacts as first-class nodes and chains them to code symbols — directly matching Gimle §5's requirement for Issue→File/Symbol linkage in the knowledge graph. 2. Neo4j-native storage makes the graph immediately compatible with Gimle's existing Neo4j infrastructure; APOC and GDS plugins are already available in palace-mcp's Neo4j service. 3. LLM-free ingest + LLM-agnostic repair interface enables cost-controlled operation ($0.20/repair) and easy substitution of any LLM without retraining.
weaknesses: 1. No temporal model — KGCompass does not track when issues were opened/closed, when files changed, or how the graph evolves over time. Gimle §5's bi-temporal requirement (valid_from/to + recorded_at) would need to be layered on top. 2. Schema is too narrow for Gimle §5's full scope — 5 node types cover issues/PRs and code-structure but omit Module, UIComponent, APIEndpoint, Decision, Iteration, and the 4-axis multi-label faceting. The schema is a starting point, not a complete foundation. 3. Research prototype with uncertain ongoing maintenance — no versioned releases, star count unknown, and the paper is from March 2025 with no confirmed subsequent major updates.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas (highest fit among the three for Gimle's cross-domain requirements). Ideas to steal: (1) Issue-PR-to-symbol chain as the core linking pattern. Gimle §5 should implement exactly this edge structure: `(:Issue)-[:TOUCHES]->(:File)`, `(:Issue)-[:TOUCHES]->(:Symbol)`, `(:PullRequest)-[:MODIFIES]->(:File)`, `(:PullRequest)-[:MODIFIES]->(:Symbol)`. KGCompass proves this chain enables multi-hop localizations that pure LLMs cannot achieve. (2) Neo4j + APOC/GDS as the graph backend — already compatible with palace-mcp's infrastructure. KGCompass's Cypher query patterns for multi-hop traversal from issue to function can be adapted directly. Example Cypher: `MATCH (i:Issue {id: $issue_id})-[:TOUCHES*1..3]->(s:Symbol) RETURN s`. (3) Top-N candidate pruning before LLM call — the top-20 function cap is a simple but effective retrieval budget control; Gimle should implement an equivalent max_candidates parameter. (4) Path-guided prompting — attaching the graph traversal path as a reasoning chain to the LLM context is a provenance pattern Gimle can adopt for Decision and Iteration nodes. Incompatibilities: KGCompass's schema has no bi-temporal fields (valid_from, valid_to, recorded_at) and no multi-label faceting — Gimle must extend all KGCompass node/edge types with these properties rather than adopting the schema verbatim.
